1:
"And I stood upon the sand of the sea, and saw a beast rise up out of the sea, having seven heads and ten horns, and upon his horns ten crowns, and upon his heads the name of blasphemy." The witnesses note the beast rising from the sea — the crux of its identity. On "saw a beast rise up out of the sea, having seven heads and ten horns," interpreters divide over the beast. The grammatical-historical Barnes, with the Reformed Gill, holds the beast is the Roman power — specifically the civil or secular Roman power (the fourth beast of Daniel 7) that supported the church of Rome; the historicist reading takes the beast as imperial/papal Rome, the persecuting world-power; the "seven heads are seven mountains" (17:9), the seat of Rome. The latin-fathers Victorinus and Bede read the beast as the kingdom of Antichrist (the Roman Empire as the seat of the final Antichrist); the preterist reads it as the Roman Empire/Nero; the futurist as a future Antichrist-kingdom.

On "ten crowns... and upon his heads the name of blasphemy," the witnesses note the marks of usurped dominion. Barnes and Ellicott note that the "ten horns" with "ten crowns" mark the beast's royal power and the kingdoms within it (the same as the dragon's, 12:3, but now crowned on the horns); "the name of blasphemy" on its heads marks its blasphemous claims and titles (the arrogating of divine honors).

On the beast's origin, the grammatical-historical note the sea: the beast rises "out of the sea" — the sea, the figure of the nations and peoples in commotion (17:15); the beast arises out of the turbulent mass of nations; the persecuting world-power emerging from the sea of peoples. The verse displays the beast rising from the sea.

The witnesses agree the substance: a blasphemous beast with seven heads and ten crowned horns rises from the sea of nations, deriving its power from the dragon (v.2) — the great persecuting world-power (Barnes, Gill, Victorinus). But its identity bears a crux: the secular/imperial Roman power supporting papal Rome (historicist — Barnes, Gill), the Roman Empire/Nero (preterist), or a future Antichrist-kingdom (futurist; Victorinus, Bede read the kingdom of Antichrist). The verse displays the beast crux. The verse marks the beast: from the sea. The verse displays the heads: seven. The verse displays the debate: Rome or Antichrist. The verse displays the blasphemy: its name. The vision displays the blasphemous world-power risen from the nations. The verse displays the beast: rising. The verse displays the horns: ten, crowned. The verse displays the blasphemy: named. The beast rising from the sea displays the opening of the great vision and a crux: a seven-headed, ten-horned beast, marked with blasphemy, rises from the sea of nations — read by the historicists as imperial/papal Rome (Barnes, Gill), by the preterist as the Roman Empire, and by the futurist and the fathers (Victorinus, Bede) as the kingdom of Antichrist. The verse displays the risen beast, empowered by the dragon (v.2).
2:
"And the beast which I saw was like unto a leopard, and his feet were as the feet of a bear, and his mouth as the mouth of a lion: and the dragon gave him his power, and his seat, and great authority." The witnesses note the composite beast empowered by the dragon. On "the beast which I saw was like unto a leopard, and his feet were as the feet of a bear, and his mouth as the mouth of a lion," the grammatical voices note the composite figure. Barnes and Robertson note the combination of Daniel's beasts: "like unto a leopard... feet of a bear... mouth of a lion" — the beast combines the features of the leopard, bear, and lion (Daniel 7:4-6, the first three beasts); it gathers into itself the characteristics of all the preceding world-empires; the figure of the most terrible, comprehensive persecuting power.

On the composite features, the witnesses note the meaning. Barnes and Ellicott note that the beast unites the swiftness/cruelty of the leopard, the strength/ferocity of the bear, and the devouring power of the lion — all the qualities of the great empires concentrated in this one beast; in the historicist reading, Rome inheriting and surpassing the powers before it.

On "the dragon gave him his power, and his seat, and great authority," the grammatical-historical note the satanic empowerment: "the dragon gave him his power, and his seat (throne), and great authority" — the beast derives its power, throne, and authority from the dragon (Satan, 12:9); the persecuting world-power is Satan's instrument, energized and authorized by him; the beast is the visible agent of the dragon's hostility to God and the church. The verse displays the composite beast empowered by the dragon.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the composite — "like unto a leopard... feet of a bear... mouth of a lion," the beast combining Daniel's three beasts, the comprehensive persecuting power (Daniel 7:4-6, Barnes, Robertson); the features uniting the qualities of the great empires (Barnes, Ellicott); "the dragon gave him his power, and his seat, and great authority," the beast empowered and authorized by Satan, his instrument (12:9, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the dragon-empowered beast. The verse marks the beast: leopard, bear, lion. The verse displays the composite: Daniel's beasts. The verse displays the power: from the dragon. The verse displays the seat: and authority. The figure displays the world-power energized by Satan. The verse displays the beast: composite. The verse displays the dragon: empowering. The verse displays the authority: given. The composite beast empowered by the dragon displays the nature and source of the persecuting power: it combines the leopard, bear, and lion (Daniel's empires) and receives its power, throne, and great authority from the dragon — Satan's visible instrument against God and the church. The verse displays the empowered beast, whose deadly wound is healed (v.3).
3:
"And I saw one of his heads as it were wounded to death; and his deadly wound was healed: and all the world wondered after the beast." The witnesses note the beast's deadly wound healed and the world's wonder. On "I saw one of his heads as it were wounded to death; and his deadly wound was healed," the grammatical voices note the wound and revival. Barnes and Robertson note the figure: "one of his heads as it were wounded to death; and his deadly wound (death-stroke) was healed" — one of the beast's heads is mortally wounded, yet the deadly wound is healed; the figure of the persecuting power suffering an apparently fatal blow and then reviving; interpreters apply it variously — the fall and revival of the Roman power, the wounding of the empire and its revival in the papal form, or (preterist) the Nero-redivivus expectation.

On "his deadly wound was healed," the witnesses note the revival. Barnes and Ellicott note that the healing of the death-stroke marks the recovery and continuation of the beast's power; in the historicist reading, the secular Roman power, wounded (by the fall of the empire), revives in a new form (papal Rome — Gill: "introducing popish idolatry, the same in substance, only in a new form"); the persecuting power survives its apparent death.

On "all the world wondered after the beast," the grammatical-historical note the universal amazement: "all the world wondered after (followed in wonder) the beast" — the whole world, astonished at the beast's revival, follows and admires it; the apparent invincibility of the revived power draws the world's wondering allegiance. The verse displays the beast's deadly wound healed and the world's wonder.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the wound — "one of his heads as it were wounded to death; and his deadly wound was healed," the persecuting power mortally wounded yet revived (Barnes, Robertson); the healing marking the recovery of the beast, the secular power reviving in the papal form to historicists (Gill, Ellicott); "all the world wondered after the beast," the world's wondering allegiance to the revived power (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the revived beast. The verse marks the head: wounded to death. The verse displays the wound: healed. The verse displays the revival: of the power. The verse displays the wonder: of the world. The vision displays the persecuting power revived and admired. The verse displays the wound: deadly. The verse displays the healing: accomplished. The verse displays the world: wondering. The beast's deadly wound healed and the world's wonder display the apparent invincibility of the persecuting power: one head is wounded to death, yet the deadly wound is healed, and all the world wonders after the beast — the power that seemed slain revives (the empire revived in papal Rome, to historicists), drawing the world's astonished allegiance. The verse displays the revived beast, worshipped with the dragon (v.4).
4:
"And they worshipped the dragon which gave power unto the beast: and they worshipped the beast, saying, Who is like unto the beast? who is able to make war with him?" The brief verse notes the worship of the dragon and the beast. The grammatical voices note the idolatrous homage. Barnes and Robertson note the double worship: "they worshipped the dragon which gave power unto the beast: and they worshipped the beast" — the world worships both the dragon (Satan, the source of the beast's power) and the beast itself; the homage paid to the persecuting power is, in effect, the worship of Satan behind it; idolatry directed to the beast and its author.

On "they worshipped the dragon which gave power unto the beast," the witnesses note the worship of Satan. Barnes and Gill note that in worshiping the beast the world worships the dragon — the devil who empowered it; the beast-worship is satanic idolatry; the homage of the world to the antichristian power is the worship of its diabolical source.

On "Who is like unto the beast? who is able to make war with him?" the grammatical-historical note the blasphemous acclamation: the worshippers cry "Who is like unto the beast? who is able to make war with him?" — a blasphemous parody of the praise due to God ("Who is like unto thee, O LORD?" Exodus 15:11); the beast's seeming invincibility and irresistible power draw an acclamation that belongs to God alone; the deification of brute power. The verse displays the worship of the dragon and the beast.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the double worship — "they worshipped the dragon which gave power unto the beast: and they worshipped the beast," the world worshiping both Satan and his beast (Barnes, Robertson); the beast-worship in effect the worship of the dragon, satanic idolatry (Barnes, Gill); "Who is like unto the beast?" the blasphemous parody of God's praise (Exodus 15:11), the deification of power (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the beast-worship. The verse marks the worship: of the dragon. The verse displays the homage: to the beast. The verse displays the cry: who is like the beast. The verse displays the blasphemy: God's praise usurped. The worship displays the world deifying the satanic power. The verse displays the dragon: worshiped. The verse displays the beast: adored. The verse displays the acclamation: blasphemous. The worship of the dragon and the beast displays the idolatry of the antichristian age: the world worships the dragon who empowered the beast, and the beast itself, crying "Who is like unto the beast? who can make war with him?" — a blasphemous parody of the praise due to God, the deification of the persecuting power. The verse displays the worshipped beast, given a blaspheming mouth (v.5).
5:
"And there was given unto him a mouth speaking great things and blasphemies; and power was given unto him to continue forty and two months." The brief verse notes the beast's blasphemous mouth and 42-month reign. The grammatical voices note the arrogant speech. Barnes and Robertson note the granted mouth: "there was given unto him a mouth speaking great things and blasphemies" — the beast is given a mouth uttering great (arrogant, boastful) things and blasphemies (drawn from Daniel 7:8, 25, the little horn's "great words against the Most High"); the beast's characteristic arrogance and blasphemy against God.

On "speaking great things and blasphemies," the witnesses note the blasphemy. Barnes and Ellicott note that the "great things and blasphemies" mark the beast's proud self-exaltation and its blasphemous claims and titles; in the historicist reading, the arrogant and blasphemous pretensions of the papal power (assuming divine titles and authority).

On "power was given unto him to continue forty and two months," the grammatical-historical note the appointed period: the beast's power is "to continue forty and two months" — the same period as elsewhere (11:2; 12:6, 14); read by historicists as 1260 years of the beast's dominion, by preterists/futurists as a literal 3.5 years; the beast's authority is real but bounded by God's appointed limit; "was given" stresses that even the beast acts only by divine permission. The verse displays the beast's blasphemous mouth and 42-month reign.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the mouth — "a mouth speaking great things and blasphemies," the beast's arrogant, blasphemous speech (Daniel 7:8, 25, Barnes, Robertson); "great things and blasphemies" the proud claims and blasphemous titles, the papal pretensions to historicists (Barnes, Ellicott); "power... forty and two months," the beast's bounded reign (= 1260 days; 11:2; 12:6), permitted by God (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the blaspheming beast. The verse marks the mouth: great things. The verse displays the speech: blasphemies. The verse displays the reign: 42 months. The verse displays the limit: appointed. The beast displays arrogant blasphemy reigning for the appointed time. The verse displays the mouth: boastful. The verse displays the blasphemy: uttered. The verse displays the months: forty-two. The beast's blasphemous mouth and 42-month reign display the character and limit of the persecuting power: it is given a mouth speaking great things and blasphemies (Daniel's little horn), and authority to continue forty-two months — the arrogant, blasphemous power reigning for the appointed period (1260 years, to historicists), yet only by God's permission. The verse displays the blaspheming beast, opening its mouth against God (v.6).
6:
"And he opened his mouth in blasphemy against God, to blaspheme his name, and his tabernacle, and them that dwell in heaven." The brief verse notes the beast's blasphemy against God, his tabernacle, and heaven. The grammatical voices note the threefold blasphemy. Barnes and Robertson note the objects of the beast's blasphemy: "he opened his mouth in blasphemy against God, to blaspheme his name, and his tabernacle, and them that dwell in heaven" — the beast blasphemes God himself, his name (his being and character), his tabernacle (his dwelling, or the church as God's dwelling), and those who dwell in heaven (the angels and the redeemed); the comprehensive blasphemy of the antichristian power against all things divine and holy.

On "to blaspheme his name, and his tabernacle," the witnesses note the targets. Barnes and Ellicott note that "his name" is God's revealed character and majesty, and "his tabernacle" is God's dwelling-place — taken as heaven, or as the church (the dwelling of God by his Spirit); the beast reviles both God and his sanctuary/people.

On "them that dwell in heaven," the grammatical-historical note the heavenly host: the beast blasphemes "them that dwell in heaven" — the angels and the glorified saints; the beast's blasphemy reaches even to the heavenly inhabitants; in the historicist reading, the papal blasphemy includes the perversion of the worship of saints and angels, or the reviling of the true heavenly church. The verse displays the beast's blasphemy against God, his tabernacle, and heaven.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the blasphemy — "he opened his mouth in blasphemy against God, to blaspheme his name, and his tabernacle, and them that dwell in heaven," the beast's comprehensive blasphemy against God, his dwelling, and the heavenly host (Barnes, Robertson); "his name... his tabernacle" God's character and dwelling/church (Barnes, Ellicott); "them that dwell in heaven," the angels and glorified saints (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the threefold blasphemy. The verse marks the blasphemy: against God. The verse displays the name: reviled. The verse displays the tabernacle: blasphemed. The verse displays the heaven-dwellers: reviled. The blasphemy displays the beast reviling all that is divine and holy. The verse displays the mouth: opened. The verse displays the blasphemy: uttered. The verse displays the targets: God, sanctuary, heaven. The beast's blasphemy against God, his tabernacle, and heaven displays the impiety of the persecuting power: it opens its mouth to blaspheme God's name, his tabernacle, and those who dwell in heaven — the comprehensive blasphemy of the antichristian power against all things holy. The verse displays the blaspheming beast, warring on the saints (v.7).
7:
"And it was given unto him to make war with the saints, and to overcome them: and power was given him over all kindreds, and tongues, and nations." The brief verse notes the beast warring on and overcoming the saints, with universal power. The grammatical voices note the persecution. Barnes and Robertson note the war on the saints: "it was given unto him to make war with the saints, and to overcome them" — the beast is permitted to war against the saints (God's people) and to overcome them (drawn from Daniel 7:21, the little horn making war with the saints and prevailing); the persecuting power triumphs, for a time, over the faithful.

On "to make war with the saints, and to overcome them," the witnesses note the apparent victory. Barnes and Ellicott note that the beast's overcoming of the saints is the outward, bodily triumph of persecution — the killing and subduing of the faithful; yet (as elsewhere) the saints overcome in spirit and faith even when bodily overcome; the beast prevails outwardly for the appointed time.

On "power was given him over all kindreds, and tongues, and nations," the grammatical-historical note the universal dominion: the beast has "power... over all kindreds, and tongues, and nations" — its authority extends over all peoples; the fourfold designation marks the universal scope of the beast's dominion; the world-wide reach of the persecuting power; yet, again, "was given" — its power is permitted and bounded by God. The verse displays the beast warring on and overcoming the saints, with universal power.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the war — "it was given unto him to make war with the saints, and to overcome them," the beast permitted to war on and overcome the saints (Daniel 7:21, Barnes, Robertson); the overcoming the outward triumph of persecution, the saints overcoming in spirit (Barnes, Ellicott); "power... over all kindreds, and tongues, and nations," the universal dominion, permitted by God (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the persecuting beast. The verse marks the war: with the saints. The verse displays the victory: overcoming them. The verse displays the dominion: over all nations. The verse displays the limit: given. The beast displays universal persecuting power over the saints. The verse displays the war: waged. The verse displays the saints: overcome. The verse displays the nations: subdued. The beast warring on and overcoming the saints, with universal power, displays the persecuting triumph of the antichristian power: it is permitted to war on the saints and overcome them, and has authority over all peoples, tongues, and nations — the world-wide persecuting dominion, yet bounded by God's permission. The verse displays the conquering beast, worshipped by all but the elect (v.8).
8:
"And all that dwell upon the earth shall worship him, whose names are not written in the book of life of the Lamb slain from the foundation of the world." The witnesses note the world worshipping the beast, except those in the Lamb's book of life. On "all that dwell upon the earth shall worship him," the grammatical voices note the universal apostasy. Barnes and Robertson note the worship of the beast: "all that dwell upon the earth shall worship him" — all the earth-dwellers (the unregenerate world) worship the beast; the persecuting power receives the homage of all, save the elect; the near-universal apostasy and idolatry of the antichristian age.

On "whose names are not written in the book of life of the Lamb," the witnesses note the exception. Barnes and Ellicott note the saving distinction: the worshippers are those "whose names are not written in the book of life of the Lamb" — only those whose names are not in the Lamb's book worship the beast; the elect, written in the book of life, are kept from the idolatry; the book of life of the Lamb distinguishes the faithful from the apostate world.

On "the Lamb slain from the foundation of the world," the eastern Cyril and grammatical-historical note the eternal purpose: the Lamb is "slain from the foundation of the world" — Christ's sacrifice, though accomplished in time, was in God's eternal purpose from the foundation of the world (or the names were written in the book from the foundation of the world); the redemption and election are grounded in God's eternal counsel; the security of those written in the book rests on the Lamb's foreordained sacrifice. The verse displays the world worshipping the beast, except those in the Lamb's book of life.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the worship — "all that dwell upon the earth shall worship him," the unregenerate world worshiping the beast, near-universal apostasy (Barnes, Robertson); "whose names are not written in the book of life of the Lamb," the elect kept from the idolatry, distinguished by the book of life (Barnes, Ellicott); "the Lamb slain from the foundation of the world," Christ's sacrifice in God's eternal purpose, the ground of election (Cyril, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the worshipping world and the kept elect. The verse marks the worship: of all the earth. The verse displays the exception: the book of life. The verse displays the Lamb: slain from eternity. The verse displays the security: the elect kept. The verse displays the world worshiping, the elect preserved. The verse displays the worship: universal. The verse displays the book: of life. The verse displays the Lamb: foreordained. The world worshipping the beast, except those in the Lamb's book of life, displays the great apostasy and the preserved remnant: all the earth-dwellers worship the beast, save those whose names are written in the book of life of the Lamb slain from the foundation of the world — the elect, secured by God's eternal purpose, kept from the idolatry. The verse displays the kept elect, called to hear (v.9).
9:
"If any man have an ear, let him hear." The very brief verse notes the call to hear. The grammatical voices note the solemn summons. Barnes and Robertson note the arresting call: "if any man have an ear, let him hear" — the same formula used in the seven letters (2:7, etc.) and by Christ in the Gospels (Matthew 11:15); here it solemnly arrests attention before the weighty word that follows (v.10); the call to heed what is said about the beast and the patience of the saints.

On the placement, the witnesses note the emphasis. Barnes and Ellicott note that the call to hear, set here (and not, as in the letters, after a promise), marks the special importance of the following declaration about captivity, the sword, and the patience of the saints; the reader is bidden to give earnest attention.

On "let him hear," the grammatical-historical note the universal address: "if any man have an ear, let him hear" — everyone who can hear is called to heed; the message about the beast and the saints' endurance is for all; the summons underlines the gravity of the warning and comfort that follow. The verse displays the call to hear.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the summons — "if any man have an ear, let him hear," the solemn formula arresting attention (2:7; Matthew 11:15, Barnes, Robertson); the call here marking the importance of the following word about the saints' patience (Barnes, Ellicott); "let him hear," the universal call to heed the weighty message (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the call to hear. The verse marks the ear: to hear. The verse displays the summons: let him hear. The verse displays the emphasis: the weighty word. The verse displays the address: to all. The call displays the reader bidden to heed the message of the beast and the saints. The verse displays the ear: opened. The verse displays the hearing: urged. The verse displays the attention: arrested. The call to hear displays the solemn summons before the word on the saints' endurance: whoever has an ear, let him hear — the reader bidden to give earnest heed to what follows about captivity, the sword, and the patience of the saints. The verse displays the arresting call, framing the saints' patience (v.10).
10:
"He that leadeth into captivity shall go into captivity: he that killeth with the sword must be killed with the sword. Here is the patience and the faith of the saints." The witnesses note the law of retribution and the patience of the saints. On "He that leadeth into captivity shall go into captivity: he that killeth with the sword must be killed with the sword," the grammatical voices note the principle of just recompense. Barnes and Robertson note the law of retribution: "he that leadeth into captivity shall go into captivity: he that killeth with the sword must be killed with the sword" — the persecutors who lead the saints into captivity and slay them with the sword shall themselves suffer the same; the principle of divine retribution (cf. Matthew 26:52; Genesis 9:6) — the beast and its agents will be repaid in kind; God's justice will requite the persecutors.

On the retribution, the witnesses note the comfort. Barnes and Ellicott note that this assurance of retribution comforts the persecuted saints — though the beast triumphs for a time, its violence will recoil on itself; the saints may endure, knowing God will avenge; the certainty of judgment on the oppressor sustains the oppressed.

On "Here is the patience and the faith of the saints," the grammatical-historical note the call to endurance: "here is the patience (patient endurance) and the faith of the saints" — this truth (of certain retribution and God's justice) is the ground and occasion of the saints' patient endurance and faith; in the time of persecution, the saints are called not to retaliate but to endure faithfully, trusting God's justice; their patience and faith are exercised and sustained by the assurance of divine retribution. The verse displays the law of retribution and the patience of the saints.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the retribution — "he that leadeth into captivity shall go into captivity: he that killeth with the sword must be killed with the sword," the persecutors repaid in kind, divine retribution (Matthew 26:52, Barnes, Robertson); the assurance comforting the persecuted, the beast's violence recoiling on itself (Barnes, Ellicott); "here is the patience and the faith of the saints," the truth grounding the saints' patient endurance and faith (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the saints' patience. The verse marks the captivity: requited. The verse displays the sword: repaid. The verse displays the retribution: divine. The verse displays the saints: patient and faithful. The principle displays the persecutors judged and the saints called to endure. The verse displays the captivity: returned. The verse displays the sword: turned. The verse displays the patience: of the saints. The law of retribution and the patience of the saints display the comfort and call amid persecution: those who lead into captivity and kill with the sword shall be so repaid — God's certain retribution on the persecutors; herein is the patient endurance and faith of the saints, called to endure rather than retaliate, trusting God's justice. The verse displays the patient saints, confronted now by the second beast (v.11).
11:
"And I beheld another beast coming up out of the earth; and he had two horns like a lamb, and he spake as a dragon." The witnesses note the second beast from the earth — the crux of its identity. On "another beast coming up out of the earth; and he had two horns like a lamb, and he spake as a dragon," interpreters divide over the second beast. The Reformed Gill and the historicist tradition (with Barnes) read the second beast as the papal/ecclesiastical power — the spiritual or religious arm of antichristian Rome: Gill identifies it with the see of Rome, the pope with his clergy, cardinals, bishops, and priests; Barnes reads it as the papal spiritual power, lamb-like in its profession of meekness yet dragon-like in its speech. The futurist reads the second beast as the false prophet (16:13; 19:20) of the end-time, the religious deceiver who serves the first beast. The verse marks both beasts as antichristian, one secular, one religious.

On "two horns like a lamb," the witnesses note the deceptive gentleness. Barnes and Ellicott note that the second beast "had two horns like a lamb" — it resembles a lamb, with the appearance of gentleness, meekness, and innocence; it claims Christlike (lamb-like) character; the false prophet's outward show of religion and mildness, masking its true nature.

On "he spake as a dragon," the eastern Cyril and grammatical-historical note the dragon-voice: though lamb-like in appearance, "he spake as a dragon" — its speech betrays its true, satanic character; the false meekness conceals a dragon's voice; the hypocrisy of the religious deceiver, gentle in show but speaking the dragon's lies; Cyril and the fathers note the antichristian deceiver who comes in sheep's clothing. The verse displays the second beast from the earth.

The witnesses agree the substance: a second beast rises from the earth, lamb-like in appearance but dragon-voiced — the deceptive religious power serving the first beast (Barnes, Gill, Cyril). Its identity bears a crux: the papal/ecclesiastical power, the pope and clergy (historicist — Gill, Barnes) vs the false prophet of the end (futurist). The verse displays the second-beast crux. The verse marks the beast: from the earth. The verse displays the horns: lamb-like. The verse displays the debate: papal power or false prophet. The verse displays the voice: a dragon's. The vision displays the deceptive religious power masking the dragon. The verse displays the beast: rising. The verse displays the lamb: counterfeited. The verse displays the dragon: speaking. The second beast from the earth displays the rise of the false prophet and a crux: a beast with lamb's horns but a dragon's voice — read by the historicists as the papal/ecclesiastical power, the pope with his clergy (Gill, Barnes), and by the futurist as the false prophet of the end; the religious deceiver, gentle in show but satanic in speech. The verse displays the false prophet, who serves and promotes the first beast (v.12).
12:
"And he exerciseth all the power of the first beast before him, and causeth the earth and them which dwell therein to worship the first beast, whose deadly wound was healed." The witnesses note the second beast enforcing worship of the first. On "he exerciseth all the power of the first beast before him," the grammatical voices note the delegated authority. Barnes and Robertson note that the second beast "exerciseth all the power of the first beast before (in the presence/on behalf of) him" — the second beast wields the full authority of the first, acting as its agent and promoter; the religious power serves the secular, exercising its authority and advancing its cause.

On "causeth the earth and them which dwell therein to worship the first beast," the witnesses note the enforced worship. Barnes and Ellicott note that the second beast "causeth the earth... to worship the first beast" — its chief work is to make the world worship the first beast; the false prophet directs the world's homage to the persecuting power; the religious deceiver enforces the idolatry of the beast.

On "whose deadly wound was healed," the grammatical-historical note the revived beast: the worship is directed to the first beast "whose deadly wound was healed" (v.3) — the beast that recovered from its mortal wound; the second beast promotes the worship of the revived persecuting power; in the historicist reading, the ecclesiastical power upholds and enforces the worship of the revived (papal) Roman power. The verse displays the second beast enforcing worship of the first.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the delegated power — "he exerciseth all the power of the first beast before him," the second beast wielding the first's authority as its agent (Barnes, Robertson); "causeth the earth... to worship the first beast," its chief work to enforce the world's worship of the beast (Barnes, Ellicott); "whose deadly wound was healed," the worship directed to the revived beast (v.3, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the enforced worship. The verse marks the power: of the first beast. The verse displays the work: enforcing worship. The verse displays the object: the first beast. The verse displays the beast: revived. The second beast displays the false prophet enforcing the beast's worship. The verse displays the authority: exercised. The verse displays the worship: enforced. The verse displays the wound: healed. The second beast enforcing worship of the first displays the work of the false prophet: it exercises all the authority of the first beast and makes the earth and its dwellers worship the first beast, whose deadly wound was healed — the religious power directing and enforcing the world's idolatrous homage to the revived persecuting power. The verse displays the enforcing beast, working great wonders (v.13).
13:
"And he doeth great wonders, so that he maketh fire come down from heaven on the earth in the sight of men," The brief verse notes the second beast's lying wonders, even fire from heaven. The grammatical voices note the deceptive miracles. Barnes and Robertson note the signs: "he doeth great wonders, so that he maketh fire come down from heaven on the earth in the sight of men" — the second beast works great signs to deceive, even making fire come down from heaven before men; the false prophet's lying wonders (cf. 2 Thessalonians 2:9; Matthew 24:24) that authenticate his deception.

On "great wonders," the witnesses note the false signs. Barnes and Gill note that the "great wonders" are the deceptive miracles by which the false prophet imposes on the world; whether real (satanic) wonders or pretended/lying signs, they serve to deceive and to validate the worship of the beast; the counterfeit of true prophetic power.

On "maketh fire come down from heaven... in the sight of men," the grammatical-historical note the parody of Elijah: the false prophet "maketh fire come down from heaven" — a parody of Elijah's calling down fire (1 Kings 18; cf. 2 Kings 1); the imitation of the genuine prophetic sign, used to deceive; the counterfeit miracle persuades the world to follow the beast. The verse displays the second beast's lying wonders, even fire from heaven.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the wonders — "he doeth great wonders, so that he maketh fire come down from heaven... in the sight of men," the false prophet's deceptive signs, even fire from heaven (2 Thessalonians 2:9, Barnes, Robertson); the "great wonders" the lying miracles that deceive and validate beast-worship (Barnes, Gill); "fire come down from heaven" a parody of Elijah's sign (1 Kings 18, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the lying wonders. The verse marks the wonders: great. The verse displays the sign: fire from heaven. The verse displays the purpose: to deceive. The verse displays the parody: of Elijah. The wonders display the false prophet deceiving by counterfeit miracles. The verse displays the wonders: worked. The verse displays the fire: called down. The verse displays the deception: advanced. The second beast's lying wonders, even fire from heaven, display the deceptive power of the false prophet: it works great signs, even making fire come down from heaven before men — the lying wonders (a parody of Elijah's sign) by which it deceives the world into worshiping the beast. The verse displays the deceiving beast, commanding an image to be made (v.14).
14:
"And deceiveth them that dwell on the earth by the means of those miracles which he had power to do in the sight of the beast; saying to them that dwell on the earth, that they should make an image to the beast, which had the wound by a sword, and did live." The witnesses note the false prophet deceiving the world and commanding an image of the beast. On "deceiveth them that dwell on the earth by the means of those miracles," the grammatical voices note the deception. Barnes and Robertson note the effect of the wonders: "deceiveth them that dwell on the earth by the means of those miracles which he had power to do in the sight of the beast" — the false prophet deceives the world by the miracles it is permitted to work in the presence of (on behalf of) the beast; the lying signs (v.13) accomplish their purpose — the world is led astray.

On "saying... that they should make an image to the beast," the witnesses note the commanded image. Barnes and Ellicott note that the false prophet commands the world to "make an image to the beast, which had the wound by a sword, and did live" — the deceived world is bidden to make an image (a likeness, representation, or institution) honoring the beast that was wounded and revived; the setting up of beast-worship in a visible image (recalling Nebuchadnezzar's image, Daniel 3); in the historicist reading, the image is read of an institution or system imposed to honor the antichristian power.

On "which had the wound by a sword, and did live," the grammatical-historical note the revived beast: the image is made to the beast "which had the wound by a sword, and did live" — again the revived beast (v.3, 12); the image honors the persecuting power that survived its mortal wound; the deception centers on the worship of the revived beast through its image. The verse displays the false prophet deceiving the world and commanding an image of the beast.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the deception — "deceiveth them that dwell on the earth by the means of those miracles," the false prophet leading the world astray by its lying signs (Barnes, Robertson); "that they should make an image to the beast," the commanded image/institution honoring the beast (Daniel 3, Barnes, Ellicott); "which had the wound by a sword, and did live," the image to the revived beast (v.3, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the commanded image. The verse marks the deception: by miracles. The verse displays the command: make an image. The verse displays the object: the beast. The verse displays the beast: wounded and revived. The deception displays the false prophet setting up beast-worship. The verse displays the world: deceived. The verse displays the image: commanded. The verse displays the beast: revived. The false prophet deceiving the world and commanding an image of the beast display the establishment of beast-worship: by its lying miracles the false prophet deceives the world and bids them make an image to the beast that was wounded by the sword and lived — the visible institution of the worship of the revived persecuting power. The verse displays the commanded image, given life and voice (v.15).
15:
"And he had power to give life unto the image of the beast, that the image of the beast should both speak, and cause that as many as would not worship the image of the beast should be killed." The witnesses note the image given life and the death-decree against non-worshippers. On "he had power to give life unto the image of the beast, that the image... should both speak," the grammatical voices note the animated image. Barnes and Robertson note the deceptive marvel: "he had power to give life (breath) unto the image of the beast, that the image of the beast should both speak" — the false prophet animates the image, giving it the appearance of life and the power of speech; the deception is heightened — the image seems alive and speaks; whether by satanic art or imposture, the image is made to seem a living, speaking thing.

On the speaking image, the witnesses note the deception. Barnes and Ellicott note that the speaking image deepens the delusion — the world is awed by an image that appears to live and speak; in the historicist reading, the giving of life and voice to the image is read of the enlivening of the institution or system of beast-worship, made to speak with authority.

On "cause that as many as would not worship the image of the beast should be killed," the grammatical-historical note the death-decree: the image is made to "cause that as many as would not worship the image of the beast should be killed" — a decree of death against all who refuse to worship the image; the enforced worship is backed by lethal compulsion (recalling Daniel 3, the fiery furnace for those who would not worship); the persecution of the faithful who will not bow to the beast. The verse displays the image given life and the death-decree against non-worshippers.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the animated image — "he had power to give life unto the image of the beast, that the image... should both speak," the false prophet animating the image to seem alive and speak (Barnes, Robertson); the speaking image deepening the delusion, the system of beast-worship given voice to historicists (Barnes, Ellicott); "cause that as many as would not worship the image... should be killed," the death-decree enforcing worship (Daniel 3, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the deadly image-worship. The verse marks the image: given life. The verse displays the speech: it spoke. The verse displays the decree: worship or die. The verse displays the persecution: of the faithful. The image displays enforced worship backed by death. The verse displays the image: living. The verse displays the speaking: marvel. The verse displays the death: decreed. The image given life and the death-decree against non-worshippers display the deadly compulsion of beast-worship: the false prophet gives the image life and speech, and decrees death for all who will not worship it — the enforced idolatry backed by lethal persecution (as in Daniel's fiery furnace) of those who refuse to bow. The verse displays the death-decree, enforced by the mark of the beast (vv.16-17).
16:
"And he causeth all, both small and great, rich and poor, free and bond, to receive a mark in their right hand, or in their foreheads:" The witnesses note the mark of the beast imposed on all. On "he causeth all, both small and great, rich and poor, free and bond," the grammatical voices note the universal imposition. Barnes and Robertson note the comprehensive scope: "he causeth all, both small and great, rich and poor, free and bond" — the false prophet imposes the mark on all classes without exception; the comprehensive pairs (small/great, rich/poor, free/bond) mark the universal reach of the requirement; none are exempt.

On "to receive a mark in their right hand, or in their foreheads," the witnesses note the mark. Barnes and Gill note that the mark (charagma) is received "in their right hand, or in their foreheads" — a visible token of allegiance to the beast, placed on the most conspicuous and active parts; a parody of the sealing of God's servants (7:3; 14:1) and of the binding of God's word on hand and forehead (Deuteronomy 6:8); the mark of subjection to the beast, professing and practicing allegiance to it.

On the mark, the grammatical-historical note the meaning: the mark signifies open allegiance to and identification with the beast — whether a literal mark, or (in the historicist/symbolic reading) the profession and badge of submission to the antichristian power; those who bear it belong to the beast, in contrast to the sealed servants of God. The verse displays the mark of the beast imposed on all.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the imposition — "he causeth all, both small and great, rich and poor, free and bond, to receive a mark," the mark imposed on all classes without exception (Barnes, Robertson); "a mark in their right hand, or in their foreheads," the visible token of allegiance, a parody of God's sealing (7:3; Deuteronomy 6:8, Barnes, Gill); the mark signifying open allegiance to the beast (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the imposed mark. The verse marks the scope: all classes. The verse displays the mark: hand or forehead. The verse displays the meaning: allegiance to the beast. The verse displays the parody: of God's seal. The mark displays universal subjection to the beast required. The verse displays the all: marked. The verse displays the hand: or forehead. The verse displays the allegiance: enforced. The mark of the beast imposed on all displays the badge of allegiance to the persecuting power: the false prophet causes all classes — small and great, rich and poor, free and bond — to receive a mark in the right hand or forehead; the token of subjection to the beast, parodying the sealing of God's servants. The verse displays the imposed mark, controlling all commerce (v.17).
17:
"And that no man might buy or sell, save he that had the mark, or the name of the beast, or the number of his name." The brief verse notes the economic coercion enforcing the mark. The grammatical voices note the boycott. Barnes and Robertson note the compulsion: "that no man might buy or sell, save he that had the mark, or the name of the beast, or the number of his name" — none may engage in commerce (buy or sell) unless they bear the mark, the name of the beast, or the number of its name; the false prophet enforces allegiance to the beast by economic coercion — exclusion from trade and livelihood for all who refuse the mark.

On "the mark, or the name of the beast, or the number of his name," the witnesses note the three forms. Barnes and Ellicott note the three equivalents: the "mark," the "name of the beast," and "the number of his name" — different forms of the same badge of allegiance; to bear any is to profess subjection to the beast; the number of his name (v.18) is a particular form of this identification.

On the economic coercion, the grammatical-historical note the pressure: by barring the unmarked from buying and selling, the beast-power compels submission through want and ruin; the faithful who refuse the mark are excluded from the ordinary means of life; the persecution operates not only by death (v.15) but by economic strangulation; in the historicist reading, the exclusion of those who would not conform to the antichristian system. The verse displays the economic coercion enforcing the mark.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the coercion — "that no man might buy or sell, save he that had the mark," none allowed to trade without the badge of the beast (Barnes, Robertson); "the mark, or the name of the beast, or the number of his name," the three equivalent forms of allegiance (Barnes, Ellicott); the economic strangulation compelling submission, excluding the faithful from livelihood (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the enforced mark. The verse marks the trade: barred without the mark. The verse displays the forms: mark, name, number. The verse displays the coercion: economic. The verse displays the faithful: excluded. The compulsion displays allegiance enforced by want. The verse displays the buying: forbidden. The verse displays the mark: required. The verse displays the exclusion: of the faithful. The economic coercion enforcing the mark displays the second instrument of compulsion: none may buy or sell without the mark, the name, or the number of the beast — the faithful who refuse it excluded from trade and livelihood, allegiance to the beast enforced by economic strangulation. The verse displays the enforced mark, whose number invites the wise to count (v.18).
18:
"Here is wisdom. Let him that hath understanding count the number of the beast: for it is the number of a man; and his number is Six hundred threescore and six." The witnesses note the number of the beast, 666 — the famous crux of its meaning. On "Here is wisdom. Let him that hath understanding count the number of the beast," the grammatical voices note the invitation to compute. Barnes and Robertson note the call: "Here is wisdom. Let him that hath understanding count (calculate) the number of the beast" — the riddle invites the wise to reckon the number; the number is a cryptogram, to be solved by computation (gematria, by which letters have numerical values); the believer is invited to discern the beast's identity through its number.

On "for it is the number of a man; and his number is Six hundred threescore and six," interpreters divide over the computation. The grammatical-historical Barnes favors "Lateinos" (Λατεινος, the Latin/Roman kingdom or man) — whose Greek letters sum to 666 (an ancient suggestion of Irenaeus); the number marks the Latin/Roman power; this fits the historicist identification of the beast with Rome. The preterist reads it as "Nero Caesar" — whose Hebrew letters (Neron Kesar) sum to 666 (and "Nero Kesar," without the final n, to 616, explaining the ancient textual variant 616); the number names the emperor Nero. Other computations have been offered (various names summing to 666); "the number of a man" means a number reckoned as men reckon, or the number of a particular man.

On "the number of a man," the grammatical-historical note the meaning: "it is the number of a man" — either the number is computed by the ordinary human method (gematria), or it is the number belonging to a particular man/the man embodying the beast; 666, falling short of the perfect seven thrice over, marks the beast as supremely imperfect, the climax of fallen, human, antichristian power. The verse displays the number of the beast, 666.

The witnesses agree the substance: the number of the beast, 666, is a cryptogram inviting the wise to compute and so discern the beast's identity (Barnes, Robertson). But the computation divides: "Lateinos," the Latin/Roman kingdom (Irenaeus, Barnes, historicist); "Nero Caesar" (the preterist gematria, explaining the 616 variant); or other proposed names. The verse displays the 666 crux. The verse marks the call: count the number. The verse displays the number: 666. The verse displays the debate: Lateinos or Nero Caesar. The verse displays the meaning: the number of a man. The number displays the beast identified by a computed cryptogram. The verse displays the wisdom: invited. The verse displays the number: reckoned. The verse displays the man: named. The number of the beast, 666, displays the famous closing riddle: the wise are invited to count the number of the beast, the number of a man, 666 — solved by gematria as "Lateinos," the Latin/Roman power (Irenaeus, Barnes, the historicist reading), or as "Nero Caesar" (the preterist reading, the variant 616 confirming the Nero computation), the number marking the supremely imperfect, antichristian power. The verse displays the numbered beast, closing the vision before the Lamb on Zion (ch.14).
